reasoning: This 24-bit sigma-delta ADC with 256 kSPS sampling rate does not meet the control thresholds under 3A001.a.5. For ADCs with 16 bits or more resolution, 3A001.a.5.e requires a sample rate greater than 65 MSPS to be controlled. At 256 kSPS (0.256 MSPS), this device is well below that threshold. Sigma-delta ADCs are typically high-resolution, lower-speed devices for precision measurement, and this commercial data acquisition component does not meet any CCL control parameters.

item_name: Quad RF-DAC / quad RF-ADC mixed-signal front-end
reasoning: The device integrates ADCs operating at 4 GSPS (4,000 MSPS). Under 3A001.a.5.a, ADCs with resolution of 12 bits or more are controlled when the adjusted update rate exceeds 1,000 MSPS. RF-ADCs at this sample rate typically have 12-14 bit resolution, and 4,000 MSPS far exceeds the 1,000 MSPS threshold. While the 12 GSPS DACs may also be controlled under 3A001.a.14 based on settling time parameters, the ADC performance clearly triggers 3A001.a.5.a control. The integrated mixed-signal front-end is classified by its most restrictive controlled component.

item_name: 6 GSPS RF digital-to-analog converter, to 5 GHz
reasoning: This 16-bit digital-to-analog converter with a 6 GSPS update rate (6,000 MWPS) is controlled under 3A001.a.5.b, which captures DACs having a resolution of 12 bits or more with an 'adjusted update rate' exceeding 1,250 MWPS. At 16-bit resolution and 6,000 MWPS, this device substantially exceeds the control threshold of 1,250 MWPS for high-resolution DACs.

item_name: Wideband 2x2 RF agile transceiver, 47 MHz-6 GHz
reasoning: This wideband RF transceiver IC (47 MHz-6 GHz, 56 MHz bandwidth) is a commercial-grade integrated transceiver similar to devices like the AD9361 family used in SDR and wireless infrastructure. While Category 3 (3A001.b) controls certain MMICs and RF components, the controlled parameters typically involve specific power output thresholds, phase noise specifications, or frequency switching times that are not indicated here. Category 5 Part 1 (5A001.b) controls for software-defined radios and frequency-agile equipment generally apply to complete systems rather than component ICs. The device's frequency range and bandwidth are consistent with commercial telecommunications applications and do not meet the specific technical thresholds (e.g., 3A001.b.2 power outputs, 3A001.b.12 synthesizer specs) that would warrant CCL listing. Absent specifications indicating controlled parameters, this commercial RF transceiver IC is subject to the EAR but not described by any CCL entry.

This 300 MHz single-core 32-bit audio DSP is evaluated against 3A001.a.5 (digital signal processors). The controlled thresholds under 3A001.a.5 require significantly higher performance levels (e.g., fixed-point DSPs exceeding 400 MMACS aggregate one-cycle execution rate, or floating-point DSPs exceeding specific GFLOPS thresholds). A single-core 300 MHz processor designed for standard audio mixing, filtering, and equalization applications would yield approximately 300 MMACS at best (assuming one MAC per cycle), which falls below controlled thresholds. The item is a commercially available audio processor without military or strategic applications, and does not meet the technical parameters for control under any CCL entry. Therefore, it is subject to the EAR but classified as EAR99.

item_name: Narrow-band sub-1 GHz ISM RF transceiver
reasoning: This sub-1 GHz ISM-band RF transceiver operates at 80-950 MHz with standard FSK/GFSK modulation. It does not employ spread spectrum or frequency hopping techniques controlled under 5A001.b.3, nor does it meet the parameters for microwave/millimeter-wave components in 3A001.b (which typically require operation above 2.7 GHz). The device is a commercial-grade transceiver for ISM-band applications such as metering and low-power data links, lacking encryption, military-band operation, or other controlled characteristics. It is subject to the EAR but not specifically described by any CCL entry.

item_name: Integrated dual-channel RF transceiver, 75 MHz-6 GHz
reasoning: This integrated RF transceiver IC is telecommunications equipment designed for wireless infrastructure and software-defined radio applications. While it operates across 75 MHz to 6 GHz with up to 200 MHz instantaneous bandwidth, it does not meet the specific control thresholds in 5A001 (e.g., 5A001.b.4 requires ultra-wideband modulation with bandwidth ≥500 MHz). The device lacks parameters triggering 3A001.b MMIC controls. As a commercial transceiver component for cellular/wireless base stations not meeting 5A001 thresholds, it falls under 5A991 as telecommunications equipment and components not controlled by 5A001.

This heterogeneous processor combining ARM Cortex-A5 and SHARC DSP cores (consistent with Analog Devices ADSP-SC5xx family) is a commercial-grade integrated circuit for audio, industrial, and signal processing applications. It does not meet the controlled thresholds under 3A001: no indication of extreme temperature ratings (above +125°C or below -55°C) per 3A001.a.1, no radiation hardening per 3A001.a.2, not manufactured from compound semiconductors per 3A001.a.3, and the description does not indicate any other specifically controlled parameters. As a standard commercial processor without controlled characteristics, it is subject to the EAR but not described by any CCL entry.

This dedicated cryptographic controller IC is designed for information security and employs AES, a symmetric algorithm using key lengths (128/192/256 bits) in excess of 56 bits, for data confidentiality functions including TLS encryption. It also incorporates ECDSA/ECDH (asymmetric algorithms based on elliptic curves) and secure key storage. Because the item performs cryptographic functions beyond authentication or digital signature (i.e., encryption for confidentiality via AES/TLS) using symmetric algorithms with keys exceeding 56 bits, it is controlled under 5A002.a.1.a. The item does not qualify for mass-market treatment under Note 3 as it is a specialized security IC component rather than a consumer end-product.

item_name: 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi + Bluetooth module, 4 MB flash
reasoning: This is a standard commercial 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi (802.11 b/g/n) and Bluetooth/BLE module with no controlled technical parameters. The operating frequency, data rates, and power levels are consistent with mass-market consumer wireless connectivity devices. It does not meet the thresholds for 5A001 (telecommunications equipment) as it lacks controlled spread spectrum or frequency hopping parameters beyond civil use exclusions. While it incorporates standard encryption (WPA/WPA2, Bluetooth encryption), these are mass-market encryption implementations that do not warrant 5A002 classification. The dual-core processor and 4 MB flash are typical of widely available IoT modules. As a commodity wireless module with no CCL-controlled specifications, it is properly classified as EAR99.

This FPGA is evaluated under ECCN 3A001.a.7, which controls field programmable logic devices. The control thresholds are: (a) maximum single-ended digital I/O exceeding 700, or (b) aggregate one-way peak serial transceiver data rate of 500 Gb/s or greater. This device has only ~107 I/O pins, well below the 700 I/O threshold, and as a low-cost device in a 144-pin LQFP package with only ~3,520 logic cells, it is unlikely to have high-speed serial transceivers meeting the 500 Gb/s threshold. Since it does not meet any controlled parameters under 3A001.a.7 or other CCL entries, it is classified as EAR99.

This secure element IC performs cryptographic functions for information security using AES-128 symmetric encryption, which employs a key length (128 bits) in excess of 56 bits, meeting the control threshold under 5A002.a.1. The ECDSA/ECDH elliptic curve cryptography and SHA-256 hashing further support classification under Category 5 Part 2 (Information Security). While commercial authentication ICs of this type may qualify for mass market treatment under Note 3 to Category 5 Part 2 (potentially reclassifying to 5A992.c), absent a specific mass market determination, the controlling ECCN based on the cryptographic parameters described is 5A002.a.1.

This 16-bit digital signal controller at 60 MIPS does not meet the controlled thresholds under 3A001.a.1. For 3A001.a.1.a.1, the peak speed is far below 5 GFLOPS. For 3A001.a.1.a.2, the ALU access width is only 16 bits, not the required 32 bits or more. There is no indication of radiation hardening, extreme temperature ratings, or military specifications that would bring it under other controlled entries. This is a standard commercial-grade microcontroller with integrated DSP capabilities suitable for general industrial applications like motor control, and it falls below all CCL technical parameters for Category 3 electronics.

This is a standard commercial-grade Layer-2 Ethernet switch operating at 10/100 Mbps speeds. It does not meet the performance thresholds or technical parameters controlled under 5A001 (telecommunications equipment), which typically requires specific high-speed or specialized capabilities. It lacks cryptographic functionality that would trigger 5A002. As a basic managed switch IC for industrial/embedded networking without controlled characteristics (no high-speed interfaces, no encryption, standard commercial speeds), it is subject to the EAR but not described by any specific CCL entry, thus classified as EAR99.

A 10/100 Mbps Ethernet PHY transceiver is a standard commercial networking component operating at very low data rates (100 Mbps maximum) using widely available IEEE 802.3 technology. It does not meet the technical parameters for any controlled entry in Category 3 (Electronics) or Category 5 (Telecommunications). It is not specially designed for military, space, or other controlled applications, and its performance characteristics fall well below the thresholds specified in entries such as 3A001 or 5A001. As a commodity-grade integrated circuit with no controlled features, it is subject to the EAR but not described by any CCL entry.

This is a standard commercial 10/100 Mbps Ethernet physical-layer transceiver, a commodity-grade networking component. It does not meet the technical thresholds for control under Category 5 Part 1 (Telecommunications) entries such as 5A001, which controls items with specific characteristics like operation at very high data rates, spread spectrum capabilities, or underwater/space qualification. It lacks cryptographic functionality that would place it under 5A002. It also does not meet Category 3 (Electronics) controlled parameters for integrated circuits under 3A001. As a widely available commercial Ethernet PHY operating at low speeds (10/100 Mbps) without any controlled technical features, the item is subject to the EAR but not specifically described on the CCL.

This microcontroller contains an integrated hardware cryptographic engine implementing AES, 3DES, and SHA algorithms with a random number generator, placing it within Category 5 Part 2 (Information Security). However, as a general-purpose commercial microcontroller with standard interfaces (CAN, USB, Ethernet) and typical specifications, it qualifies as a mass market encryption component under 5A992.c rather than the more restrictive 5A002. The crypto functionality is ancillary to the MCU's general-purpose computing role, and such commercial MCUs with integrated crypto accelerators are widely available consumer/industrial items that meet the mass market criteria in Note 3 to Category 5 Part 2.

This Bluetooth Low Energy 4.2 radio module is a standard commercial 2.4 GHz wireless transceiver using widely available, mass-market technology. It operates in the unlicensed ISM band with standard BLE frequency-hopping spread spectrum and does not meet the technical thresholds for control under 5A001 (telecommunications equipment with specific spread spectrum or encryption parameters). The module's specifications indicate consumer-grade connectivity applications without any specialized military, intelligence, or high-performance characteristics that would warrant CCL listing. As a mass-market telecommunications component subject to the EAR but not described by any specific CCL entry, it is properly classified as EAR99.

reasoning: This system-on-module is a general-purpose computer based on a quad-core ARM Cortex-A72 processor. The module's specifications (consumer-grade processor, commercial interfaces, standard form factor) are consistent with widely available single-board computers and compute modules that are subject to the EAR but not listed on the CCL.

This ARM Cortex-M4 microcontroller at 168 MHz is a general-purpose commercial embedded processor. It does not meet the performance thresholds specified in 3A001.a.1 for controlled microprocessor/microcontroller microcircuits, which require significantly higher computational performance (measured in Weighted TeraFLOPS) or specialized characteristics such as radiation hardening, extended temperature operation, or cryptographic capabilities. The Cortex-M4's modest 32-bit architecture with single-precision FPU at 168 MHz represents commodity-level technology widely available in commercial products. It also does not fall under 3A991 as it lacks the specific characteristics captured by that entry. As a mass-market microcontroller for standard embedded applications, it is subject to the EAR but not described by any CCL entry.

This 1-inch broadband dielectric mirror for visible wavelengths (400-750 nm) is a standard commercial optical component. It does not meet the controlled thresholds in 6A004 (no space qualification, not a deformable mirror, no controlled lightweight substrate specifications) or 6A005 (no controlled laser damage threshold or other laser-specific parameters specified). The fused silica substrate lacks the specific controlled thermal/homogeneity parameters of 6C004.e. As a conventional folding/turning mirror for visible-band beam steering without controlled technical characteristics, it is subject to the EAR but not described by any CCL entry.

This is a standard commercial kinematic mirror mount with typical laboratory specifications (two-adjuster tip/tilt, 1-inch optic capacity, micrometer screws, spring-loaded retention). While optical components can fall under Category 6 (Sensors and Lasers), the CCL entries such as 6A004 control specific optical elements (mirrors, lenses, coatings) and precision components meeting defined parameters, not general-purpose mechanical mounting hardware. The described mount lacks characteristics that would bring it under control—no extreme angular resolution specifications, space qualification, radiation hardening, or design for a specifically controlled system. It is a widely available general-purpose laboratory positioning device subject to the EAR but not described by any CCL entry.

The ARM Cortex-A8 is a general-purpose microprocessor. Under 3A001.a.1, microprocessors are controlled only if they exceed specific performance thresholds (e.g., high CTP values, certain aggregate bidirectional transfer rates, or extended temperature ratings). A single-core 32-bit processor at 1 GHz with integrated graphics for commercial embedded/HMI applications does not meet the controlled performance parameters in 3A001.a.1.a (CTP thresholds) or other subparagraphs. No extended temperature range or specialized military/space hardening is indicated. This is a widely available commercial processor class that does not fall under any specific CCL entry, hence EAR99.
